case_metadata.court: Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals
case_metadata.project_type: fire system replacement at naval facility

nodes.description: Navy failed to provide sufficient escorts for secure building access, with escorts frequently late or not appearing, delaying work progress.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy did not supply sufficient escorts, or escorts were late or failed to appear, delaying KUNJ's work.

nodes.description: Navy's sanitization efforts (covering sensitive machinery) precluded KUNJ from working, causing delays.
nodes.source_excerpt: KUNJ was delayed when precluded from working because of Navy's sanitization efforts.

nodes.description: Navy caused delays through building access issues and other activities that prevented KUNJ from completing work as scheduled.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy responsible for delays caused by building access issues and other Navy activities preventing work completion.

nodes.description: Navy delayed providing necessary authorization to transmitter manufacturer to sell units to KUNJ's subcontractor.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy delayed authorization to manufacturer to sell radio frequency transmitters to subcontractor.

nodes.description: Navy delayed approving use of a PED camera, causing KUNJ significant loss of time.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy delayed approving use of PED camera, causing significant time loss.

nodes.description: Navy directed relocation of conduit that KUNJ had installed in a Navy-approved location.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy directed relocation of conduit installed in Navy-approved location.

nodes.description: Navy delayed allowing KUNJ access to Building 20, then delayed implementing after-hours work arrangement due to funding issues.
nodes.source_excerpt: Navy delayed access to Building 20, then delayed implementing after-hours work due to funding issues in October 2018.

extraction_notes: All nodes in this part are alleged by KUNJ but not yet proven or adjudicated. The Navy disputes these claims and raises affirmative defenses of accord and satisfaction through modifications.

Part 2 primarily contains contract specifications, security requirements, and legal arguments about accord and satisfaction defenses rather than new delay events. No new causal events matching the predefined event_types were found in this section.

Part 3 focuses on legal arguments about release clauses and summary judgment motions rather than describing new causal events. The text discusses whether previous delay claims (referenced in nodes n1-n7) are barred by contractual modifications, but does not introduce new delay events or causal relationships.

Part 4 contains only legal arguments and procedural rulings about contract interpretation and documentation requirements, with no new factual events or causal relationships described that would require node or edge extraction
